text: The liver is retracted, and the da Vinci XI robotic system is docked, preparing for the robotic sleeve gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon operates using two arms of the da Vinci robot, approaching from the patient's right side, while the assistant positions instruments from the patient's left side.
The surgeon removes the gastrosophia fat pad, located between the gastric lumen and the left crux of the diaphragm, using robotic instruments.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to take down tissue, providing adequate exposure of the angle of His for precise dissection of the stomach, ensuring the fundus is not excessively retained.
The surgeon begins using a robotic vessel sealing device to seal and cut through small blood vessels and surrounding tissue during the robotic sleeve gastrectomy.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to dissect through the gastrosophia fat pad, enhancing exposure of the angle of His for further dissection.
The surgeon has completely removed the gastroesophageal fat pad using the da Vinci robotic system, and it is now being extracted from the patient's body.
The surgeon redirects attention to the lower stomach and begins dissection along the greater curvature, preparing to separate the stomach tissue from surrounding structures.
The surgeon identifies the pylorus through palpation and visualization, then locates an area on the lesser curve of the stomach approximately four to six centimeters proximal to the pylorus.
The surgeon begins transecting the greater curved vessels using a robotic vessel sealing device, cutting through the tissue along the greater curvature of the stomach.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealing device to seal the greater curve vessels along the stomach, ensuring hemostasis at the chosen starting point.
The surgeon begins entering the lesser sac behind the stomach, preparing to access deeper anatomical structures for further dissection.
The surgeon opens the space behind the stomach by sealing vessels and cutting through connective tissue, facilitating access to the lesser sac.
The surgeon enters the posterior space and begins rapidly dissecting the short gastric vessels along the greater curvature of the stomach.

The spleen is visible in the surgical field, with the short gastric vessels situated closely between the stomach and the spleen. The surgeon proceeds cautiously to avoid tearing these small vessels, which could lead to significant bleeding and difficulty in controlling them if they retract into the spleen.
The surgeon continues transecting the short gastric vessels along the greater curve of the stomach, ensuring clear mobilization of the stomach without bleeding in the splenic bed.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the final attachments between the posterior aspect of the stomach, revealing the left cruciate diaphragm. This ensures a clear view for establishing an adequate staple line without retaining any part of the stomach fundus.
The surgeon cauterizes attachments on the posterior side of the stomach, ensuring complete mobilization for the placement of the stapling device to perform the sleeve gastrectomy.

The surgeon passes a 34 French sizing tube down the esophagus and into the antrum of the stomach under direct vision, following the complete mobilization of the short gastric vessels and freeing of the posterior stomach attachments.
The surgeon begins creating the staple line along the stomach using a robotic stapling device, with the sizing tube positioned inside the stomach to guide the formation of the sleeve gastrectomy.
The surgeon begins using a green-colored stapler with a large staple size to create the staple line for the sleeve gastrectomy, ensuring the stomach is properly aligned and prepared for stapling.
The surgeon prepares to fire the first two staples in the thicker portion of the stomach, with the sizing tube positioned down into the stomach.
The surgeon passes a sizing tube into the antrum, the lower part of the stomach intended to be preserved, and prepares to use a robotic stapling device to initiate the sleeve gastrectomy.
The bougie is visible within the stomach, and the staple line is adequately placed along the gastric tissue using the robotic stapling device.

The surgeon uses robotic technology to measure tissue thickness and ensure adequate compression for proper staple formation along the gastric tissue. The robotic stapling device is visible as it aids in the gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon fires the robotic stapler along the stomach, causing the device to unclamp and disengage. This process is repeated vertically along the stomach tissue.
The surgeon inspects the staple line along the stomach, observing that it is perfectly formed with no visible bleeding or serosal tears.
The surgeon continues the vertical staple line using two large staplers initially, then transitions to a smaller staple line as the stomach wall becomes thinner further up.

The surgeon passes the final staple line across the apex of the stomach, leaving a small dog ear of stomach tissue. The staple line is carefully positioned to ensure it crosses the stomach and not the esophagus, which is visible to the left.

The surgeon inspects the complete staple line of the gastric sleeve, ensuring its integrity. Using a robotic needle holder, the surgeon begins to over-sew the staple line in a running inverting fashion to reinforce it.
The surgeon begins at the dog ear of the stomach above the staple line, anchoring the Tsuji and inverting the staple line using a running inverting technique.
The surgeon completes the over-sewing of the suture line for the robotic sleeve gastrectomy, ensuring the staple line is securely reinforced.
The surgeon uses an interrupted vial suture to tack the small dog-ear of the stomach at the apex to the left crux of the diaphragm, preventing the gastric sleeve from herniating into the mediastinum.
The surgeon ties the final suture and prepares to perform an endoscopic test on the sleeve gastrectomy, visually inspecting for any signs of intragastric bleeding or leaks.

The surgeon cuts the final suture and removes the stitch, followed by the removal of the thirty-four French bougie. An upper endoscopy is then performed to inspect the gastric sleeve for any bleeding or leaks.
The surgeon leaves the robotic console to perform an upper endoscopy, while the assistant remains in the surgical field.
The surgeon covers the suture line area with saline solution to visually inspect for any small air leaks, ensuring the integrity of the sleeve gastrectomy.
The gastric sleeve and antrum are visibly distended as the light from the endoscope passes through the sleeve into the antrum. There is no visible evidence of air leaks.
The surgeon suctions the residual air out of the gastric sleeve, and the endoscope is then carefully removed from the patient's stomach.

The surgeon applies fibrin sealant along the suture line to ensure closure, addressing a small serosal tear on the liver with additional sealant. The visual field shows no evidence of bleeding, and the gastric specimen is prepared for removal.
The surgeon removes the liver retractor, indicating the conclusion of the gastrectomy procedure.
